message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: No lockfile / constraints / hashes — supply-chain audit and installs are non-reproducible
DIMENSION: dependency & supply chain
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: pyproject.toml 38-74
DESCRIPTION: Pass-1 finding #8 added pip-audit to CI but left the reproducibility half of its own suggested fix unaddressed: there is still NO lockfile (no uv.lock/poetry.lock), NO constraints*.txt, NO requirements*.txt, and the audit job runs `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` resolving fresh versions every run. I confirmed the heavy extras pull a large, fast-moving surface (217+ installed dists; current resolution includes cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, urllib3 2.7.0, Werkzeug 3.1.8, Flask 3.1.3, grpcio 1.81.1, protobuf 6.33.6, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51). Because nothing is pinned, (a) the pip-audit result is a point-in-time snapshot that can differ between two CI runs on the same commit, (b) a green audit today gives no guarantee about the artifact a user installs tomorrow, and (c) pip-audit's hash/pinned-aware checks (`--require-hashes`, `--locked`) cannot be used. pip-audit clean today (OSV and PyPI both: 'No known vulnerabilities found') — but that is exactly the transient guarantee the lockfile gap undermines.
SUGGESTED FIX: Commit a locked/constraints file for the heavy lanes (e.g. `uv pip compile` or `pip-compile` output, or `uv.lock`) and have the audit job install from it and run `pip-audit -r <locked-file> --require-hashes -S`, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers. This makes both installs and the advisory scan reproducible.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding about the lack of lockfile/constraints/hashes for reproducible supply-chain audits.

content.content:
1	[build-system]
2	requires = ["hatchling"]
3	build-backend = "hatchling.build"
4	
5	[project]
6	name = "assay-engine"
7	dynamic = ["version"]  # single-sourced from src/assay_engine/__init__.py:__version__ (#120)
8	description = "Dataset-agnostic engine for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data."
9	readme = "README.md"
10	requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"
11	license = "Apache-2.0"
12	license-files = ["LICENSE"]
13	authors = [{ name = "thehipsterciso" }]
14	keywords = ["empirical", "reproducibility", "methodology", "security", "privacy", "ml", "nlp"]
15	classifiers = [
16	    "Development Status :: 4 - Beta",
17	    "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
18	    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
19	    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
20	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
21	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11",
22	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
23	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
24	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
25	    "Topic :: Scientific/Engineering",
26	    "Topic :: Security",
27	    "Typing :: Typed",
28	]
29	# The engine core is dependency-free; heavy backends are optional extras (lazy-imported,
30	# ADR-0006). The composed runner and methodology work with no extras installed.
31	dependencies = []
32	
33	[project.urls]
34	Homepage = "https://github.com/thehipsterciso/hc-assay"
35	Repository = "https://github.com/thehipsterciso/hc-assay"
36	Issues = "https://github.com/thehipsterciso/hc-assay/issues"
37	
38	[project.optional-dependencies]
39	dev = [
40	    "pytest>=8",
41	    "pytest-cov>=5",  # CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` (#103)
42	    "ruff>=0.6",
43	    "mypy>=1.11",
44	]
45	# Heavy backends for the reasoning seam — installed only where real LLM calls run.
46	# The engine imports and unit-tests (mocking the backends) without these.
47	reasoning = [
48	    "langchain-ollama>=1.1,<2",
49	    "claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3",
50	    "anyio>=4,<5",
51	]
52	observability = [
53	    "opentelemetry-sdk>=1.25,<2",
54	    "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp>=1.25,<2",
55	    "arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18",
56	    "openinference-instrumentation-langchain>=0.1.40,<1",
57	    "mlflow>=3.1,<4",
58	]
59	persistence = [
60	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",  # core — provides MemorySaver (declared, not left transitive)
61	    "langgraph-checkpoint-postgres>=3.1,<4",
62	    "psycopg[binary,pool]>=3.2,<4",
63	    "qdrant-client>=1.9,<2",
64	]
65	orchestration = [
66	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",
67	]
68	baseline = [
69	    "numpy>=1.26,<3",  # vectorized similarity at corpus scale (pure-Python fallback otherwise)
70	]
71	# Every backend in one shot (for a full local install). dev tooling stays separate.
72	all = [
73	    "assay-engine[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]",
74	]
75	
76	[tool.hatch.version]
77	path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"
78	
79	[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
80	packages = ["src/assay_engine"]
81	
82	[tool.pytest.ini_options]
83	testpaths = ["tests"]
84	pythonpath = ["src"]
85	
86	[tool.ruff]
87	line-length = 100
88	src = ["src", "tests"]
89	
90	[tool.mypy]
91	# No fixed python_version: mypy infers the target from the running interpreter, so the CI
92	# matrix (3.11–3.14) type-checks against each version rather than only 3.11 (#121).
93	packages = ["assay_engine"]
94	strict = true
95	
96	# Optional reasoning/observability backends are not installed in the type-check env; their
97	# imports are lazy and guarded at runtime, so treat them as untyped third-party modules.
98	[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
99	module = [
100	    "langchain_ollama.*",
101	    "claude_agent_sdk.*",
102	    "anyio.*",
103	    "opentelemetry.*",
104	    "phoenix.*",
105	    "mlflow.*",
106	    "openinference.*",
107	    "langgraph.*",
108	    "qdrant_client.*",
109	    "psycopg.*",
110	    "psycopg_pool.*",
111	    "numpy.*",
112	]
113	ignore_missing_imports = true
114

content.text: Confirmed: no lockfile, constraints, or requirements files exist. Let me examine the CI workflow to verify the audit job claims.

.github/workflows/ci.yml
---DOCS---
ARCHITECTURE.md
CHARTER.md
GLOSSARY.md
GOVERNANCE.md
METHODOLOGY.md
decisions
hardening
protocol
---ADRs---
./docs/decisions/ADR-0008-engine-enforced-adjudication-firewall.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0007-content-addressed-data-versioning.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0004-three-verdict-adjudication.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0005-two-firewalls.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0002-engine-adapter-blueprint.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0003-local-first-data-sovereign.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0006-optional-backends-lazy-imports.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0010-engine-composed-study-runner.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0001-independent-blind-baseline.md
./docs/decisions/ADR-0009-engine-enforced-preregistration.md

1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras)
22	        run: |
23	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
24	          pip install -e ".[dev]"
25	      - name: Lint (ruff)
26	        run: ruff check src tests
27	      - name: Format check (ruff)
28	        run: ruff format --check src tests
29	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
30	        run: mypy
31	      - name: Test (pytest)
32	        run: pytest -q
33	
34	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
35	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
36	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
37	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
38	  # e2e) run for real.
39	  integration:
40	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
41	    services:
42	      postgres:
43	        image: postgres:16
44	        env:
45	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
46	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
47	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
48	        options: >-
49	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
50	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
51	      qdrant:
52	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
53	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
54	    env:
55	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
56	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
57	    steps:
58	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
59	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
60	        with:
61	          python-version: "3.12"
62	      - name: Install (all extras)
63	        run: |
64	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
65	          pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"
66	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
67	        run: mypy
68	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
69	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
70	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
71	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
72	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
73	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
74	
75	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
76	  # with advisories). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id per line) covers
77	  # accepted/unfixable ones. (#108)
78	  audit:
79	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
80	    steps:
81	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
82	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
83	        with:
84	          python-version: "3.12"
85	      - name: Install (all extras) + pip-audit
86	        run: |
87	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit
88	          pip install -e ".[dev,all]"
89	      - name: pip-audit (known-vulnerability scan)
90	        run: |
91	          IGNORES=""
92	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
93	            while read -r id; do [ -n "$id" ] && IGNORES="$IGNORES --ignore-vuln $id"; done < .pip-audit-ignore
94	          fi
95	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES
96

input.command: cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && ls docs/hardening 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; grep -rn "pip-audit\|lockfile\|lock file\|constraints\|require-hashes\|reproducib" docs/hardening docs/decisions 2>/dev/null | head -40
input.description: List hardening docs and search for lockfile/pip-audit discussion

content.content:
CAMPAIGN.md
PASS-1.md
---
docs/decisions/ADR-0007-content-addressed-data-versioning.md:42:  reproducibility guarantee rests on engine code, not an external binary's configuration.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:19:| 8 | MEDIUM | ops-packaging-supplychain | No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine | `ci.yml` 8 |
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:31:| 20 | LOW | ops-packaging-supplychain | Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest | `pyproject.toml` 7 |
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:114:- **Verifier 1:** The mechanism is real: src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:112 (`return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]`) boxes the dense numpy similarity matrix into a nested Python list, and the public API (cosine_similarity_matrix, line 115) is contractually list[list[float]] with no streaming/top-k/blocked alternative, so a full O(n^2) dense matrix is always materialized regardless of caller need. My PoC confirms the boxing loop (0.46s) dwarfs the actual vectorized numeric work (0.019s) at n=3000, and the nested list does cost ~4x an equivalent ndarray.\n\nBut the finding is materially over-stated, and its severity rests on a flawed benchmark:\n\n1) The "~70x peak memory" headline and "~576 MB nested list" are wrong. Measured deep size of the returned nested list at n=3000,d=384 is 294 MB vs 72 MB for the equivalent ndarray = 4.1x, not 70x. (4.1x is the textbook boxed-float-vs-float64 factor.) The "70x" appears to conflate per-cell byte ratios with something else; it is not reproducible.\n\n2) The "7.76 s" runtime (and "minutes of boxing on a Mac mini") is a tracemalloc artifact. Under tracemalloc the function takes ~7.9s; with tracemalloc off, clean wall-clock is 0.33s at n=3000 an
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:140:### 8. [MEDIUM] No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:142:- **Problem:** Neither pyproject.toml nor ci.yml contains any dependency-vulnerability scanning step (`grep -n 'pip-audit\|safety\|bandit'` returns nothing). The project pulls a large, fast-moving transitive surface through its extras — verified by resolving the `observability` extra alone, which installs 150+ packages including cryptography, aiohttp, Flask/Werkzeug, requests, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, and SQLAlchemy. With only floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. `cryptography` unconstrained as a transitive, `aiohttp` transitive, `requests` transitive), a clean install on any future day can pull a transitive version with a freshly-disclosed CVE and CI will never notice. For a tool that brands itself for security & privacy data and is being hardened for production, the absence of an advisory-database check (and of a lockfile / constraints file for the heavy lanes) is a genuine supply-chain gap rather than a style preference.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:143:- **Suggested fix:** Add a CI job (or step in the integration lane) that runs `pip-audit` against the installed environment, failing on known vulnerabilities (with an allowlist file for accepted/unfixable advisories). Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:148:2. `grep -rniE 'pip-audit|safety|bandit|cyclonedx|osv|trivy|grype'` across .github/, pyproject.toml, docs/, scripts/, CONTRIBUTING.md returns nothing relevant (only unrelated "auditable provenance" mentions). The dev extra (pyproject lines 39-43) is just pytest/ruff/mypy.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:149:3. No lockfile or constraints file exists anywhere in the repo (searched for *.lock, constraints*.txt, requirements*.txt). Dependencies use floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. cryptography is not even directly declared — it is an unconstrained transitive; aiohttp/requests/urllib3 likewise transitive).
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:151:- **Verifier 2:** Independently confirmed. The repo has exactly one CI workflow (/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml) and a repo-wide grep for pip-audit|safety|bandit|trivy|snyk|osv|dependabot across all yml/yaml/toml/txt/cfg/ini files returns nothing. There is no .github/dependabot.yml, no lockfile (uv.lock), no constraints*.txt, and no requirements*.txt anywhere in the working tree (the only "audit"/".lock" hits are git branch refs and transcript JS filenames). So no dependency-vulnerability scanning and no pinned/reproducible install exist for the heavy lanes.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:157:- **Problem:** GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." That partial trail is auditable for what *succeeded*, but it records NOTHING about the failure itself: when any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, GateError, or an adapter exception), control leaves through the bare try/finally at lines 269/522 and the finally block only calls tracker.end_run — it never records a provenance entry for the abort. PoC against the venv (BadParser raising during INGEST, caller-owned trail): the trail ends at seq 0 'run_start' and seq 1 'phase enter INGEST', then stops. An auditor reading the trail alone cannot distinguish "run aborted at ingest because the parser hit disk corruption" from "run is still in progress" or "run completed but stopped logging" — the single most diagnostic fact (what failed and why) is absent precisely on the failure path the partial-trail feature exists to serve. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of diagnosing a blocked run; a silent truncation is an audit hole, not an audit record.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:247:- **Problem:** METHODOLOGY.md §6 and the fence.py docstring state the fence "refuses at construction to hold an Interpretation (even nested)" and the Measurement.__post_init__ comment claims it is blocked "at the top level OR nested in a container/metadata (issues #22, #30)." The recursive guard _contains_interpretation does NOT honor that for two cases, both reproducible against the venv:
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:270:- **Verifier 1:** The technical claim is correct and reproducible. In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py, `_submit_bounded` (lines 147-165) wraps `_pool.submit(fn)` in `try/except BaseException` solely to roll back the `_inflight` counter, then re-raises the original exception raw (line 163). When the shared module-global ThreadPoolExecutor (`_pool`, line 136) is shut down, `submit` raises `RuntimeError('cannot schedule new futures after shutdown')`, which is NOT a member of the documented `ReasoningError` hierarchy (note: `ReasoningError` subclasses `RuntimeError`, but a plain `RuntimeError` is not a `ReasoningError`, so the `except ReasoningError` clauses in `_run_with_retries` lines 448-459 do not catch it). I verified the leak at three levels: `_with_timeout`, `_submit_bounded`, and the public `TieredReasoningSeam.run()` — in all cases a bare `RuntimeError` escapes after `seam._pool.shutdown()`. A caller catching only the documented `ReasoningError` hierarchy would miss it. So the finding is genuine.\n\nSeverity is recalibrated from MEDIUM down to LOW because the trigger window is narrow and not exercised by normal operation: I grepped the whole src tree and there is NO explicit `_pool.shutdown(
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:271:- **Verifier 2:** The finding is technically accurate and reproducible. In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py, `_submit_bounded` (line 147-165) calls `_pool.submit(fn)` at line 159 inside a `try/except BaseException` that only decrements `_inflight` and re-raises the raw exception (lines 160-163). If the module-global `_pool` (line 136) is shut down, `ThreadPoolExecutor.submit` raises `RuntimeError('cannot schedule new futures after shutdown')`, which is NOT in the documented error hierarchy. Critically, this `RuntimeError` is the PARENT of `ReasoningError` (line 65: `class ReasoningError(RuntimeError)`), so the retry loop's `except ReasoningError` clauses in `_run_with_retries` (lines 448-459) do NOT catch it — it propagates straight out of the public `TieredReasoningSeam.run()`. The module docstring and class docstrings advertise `PermanentReasoningError`/`RateLimitError`/`ReasoningError` as the contract, so a caller catching that hierarchy would miss this. The suggested fix (normalize submit-time RuntimeError to PermanentReasoningError) is correct and trivial.\n\nSeverity is recalibrated DOWN from MEDIUM to LOW because the trigger surface is very narrow: I confirmed via grep that the reasoning `_
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:283:Not constraint-justified: the hard constraints (loopback-only, no metered API, engine-no-adapter-imports, lazy heavy backe
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:319:### 20. [LOW] Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:321:- **Problem:** `pyproject.toml` sets `version = "0.0.1"` (line 7) while `src/assay_engine/__init__.py` separately hardcodes `__version__ = "0.0.1"` (line 28). These are two independent strings with no single source of truth — hatchling is not configured for dynamic versioning (`grep dynamic`/`hatch.version` returns nothing). This is not merely a style nit: `src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` (line 24) and stamps it into the determinism/provenance record as `component_versions["engine"]` (line 111), which is part of the hash-chained reproducibility trail. A release that bumps only one of the two strings (the common failure mode) ships a wheel whose distributed version disagrees with the version recorded in every provenance manifest it produces — silently undermining the project's central reproducibility guarantee while passing all tests.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:327:3. The module `__version__` is consumed by the reproducibility harness: determinism.py:24 imports it as `_ENGINE_VERSION` and line 111 stamps it as `component_versions["engine"]` in the DeterminismRecord.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:348:This is a genuine (if low-impact) ops/CI correctness gap, not a style nit and not justified by any hard constraint (the local-first/no-metered-API/lazy-backend constraints are unrelated to the mypy analysis target). It is not already-handled: there is no per-version `mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}` invocation anywhere (grep found none).
docs/decisions/ADR-0003-local-first-data-sovereign.md:24:- The operator owns retention and the full reproducibility record locally.

content.content:
===PASS-1 #8 resolution===
### 8. [MEDIUM] No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine
- **Location:** `.github/workflows/ci.yml` 8
- **Problem:** Neither pyproject.toml nor ci.yml contains any dependency-vulnerability scanning step (`grep -n 'pip-audit\|safety\|bandit'` returns nothing). The project pulls a large, fast-moving transitive surface through its extras — verified by resolving the `observability` extra alone, which installs 150+ packages including cryptography, aiohttp, Flask/Werkzeug, requests, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, and SQLAlchemy. With only floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. `cryptography` unconstrained as a transitive, `aiohttp` transitive, `requests` transitive), a clean install on any future day can pull a transitive version with a freshly-disclosed CVE and CI will never notice. For a tool that brands itself for security & privacy data and is being hardened for production, the absence of an advisory-database check (and of a lockfile / constraints file for the heavy lanes) is a genuine supply-chain gap rather than a style preference.
- **Suggested fix:** Add a CI job (or step in the integration lane) that runs `pip-audit` against the installed environment, failing on known vulnerabilities (with an allowlist file for accepted/unfixable advisories). Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers.
- **Verifier 1:** Confirmed as a genuine production-readiness ops/supply-chain gap, not a false positive or a justified design choice.

What I verified:
1. CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml) has two jobs (core, integration) and runs only ruff, ruff format, mypy, and pytest. There is NO dependency-vulnerability scanning step. (Read full 74-line file.)
2. `grep -rniE 'pip-audit|safety|bandit|cyclonedx|osv|trivy|grype'` across .github/, pyproject.toml, docs/, scripts/, CONTRIBUTING.md returns nothing relevant (only unrelated "auditable provenance" mentions). The dev extra (pyproject lines 39-43) is just pytest/ruff/mypy.
3. No lockfile or constraints file exists anywhere in the repo (searched for *.lock, constraints*.txt, requirements*.txt). Dependencies use floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. cryptography is not even directly declared — it is an unconstrained transitive; aiohttp/requests/urllib3 likewise transitive).
4. The heavy extras surface is real and large: the venv with extras installed has 217 packages, and I confirmed every security-sensitive package the assessor named is actually present: cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, Flask 3.1.3, Werkzeug 3.1.8, protobuf 6.33.6, grpcio 1.81.1, o
- **Verifier 2:** Independently confirmed. The repo has exactly one CI workflow (/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml) and a repo-wide grep for pip-audit|safety|bandit|trivy|snyk|osv|dependabot across all yml/yaml/toml/txt/cfg/ini files returns nothing. There is no .github/dependabot.yml, no lockfile (uv.lock), no constraints*.txt, and no requirements*.txt anywhere in the working tree (the only "audit"/".lock" hits are git branch refs and transcript JS filenames). So no dependency-vulnerability scanning and no pinned/reproducible install exist for the heavy lanes.

The transitive-surface claim is verified against the live venv: 217 installed packages, including cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, Flask 3.1.3, Werkzeug 3.1.8, requests 2.34.2, protobuf 6.33.6, grpcio 1.81.1, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51, mlflow 3.13.0, arize-phoenix 17.6.0 — all pulled transitively via the observability/persistence/reasoning extras in pyproject.toml. The integration lane (ci.yml lines 62-65) does a fresh, unpinned `pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"` on every run, so a future CVE'd transitive can land silently with n

### 9. [MEDIUM] A raised run leaves a partial provenance trail with no failure entry — the abort reason is unrecoverable from the trail
- **Location:** `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py` 269-528 (try/finally around the phase machine; finally at 522-527 records nothing)
- **Problem:** GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." That partial trail is auditable for what *succeeded*, but it records NOTHING about the failure itself: when any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, GateError, or an adapter exception), control leaves through the bare try/finally at lines 269/522 and the finally block only calls tracker.end_run — it never records a provenance entry for the abort. PoC against the venv (BadParser raising during INGEST, caller-owned trail): the trail ends at seq 0 'run_start' and seq 1 'phase enter INGEST', then stops. An auditor reading the trail alone cannot distinguish "run aborted at ingest because the parser hit disk corruption" from "run is still in progress" or "run completed but stopped logging" — the single most diagnostic fact (what failed and why) is absent precisely on the failure path the partial-trail feature exists to serve. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of diagnosing a blocked run; a silent truncation is an audit hole, not an audit record.
- **Suggested fix:** Wrap the phase machine body in try/except/finally: on any exception, before re-raising, record a terminal entry e.g. trail.record('run_failed', f'{type(exc).__name__} during {visited[-1].name if visited else "start"}', error_type=type(exc).__name__, message=str(exc), last_phase=visited[-1].name if visited else None). Keep it inside the chain (so verify_records still passes) and guard the record() call itself so a provenance failure during teardown does not mask the original exception. This makes the partial trail self-describing: it ends with an explicit, chained 'this is why the run stopped' entry.
- **Verifier 1:** CONFIRMED as a real production-readiness defect, with a severity recalibration from HIGH to MEDIUM.

content.text:
Now I have a complete and accurate understanding. Let me confirm the precise scope of what Pass-1 #8 actually fixed.

The key facts are clear:

1. **Pass-1 #8's suggested fix had two parts**: (a) add pip-audit scanning to CI — "Add a CI job... that runs pip-audit", and (b) a softer "Consider also committing a constraints/lock file... for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited."

2. **What was actually fixed**: The `audit` job (ci.yml lines 78-95) was added, running `pip-audit` with an `.pip-audit-ignore` allowlist mechanism. This addresses part (a).

3. **What was NOT fixed**: No lockfile/constraints/requirements file exists (my find confirmed zero results). The `audit` job does `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` (unpinned, fresh resolution) and runs bare `pip-audit` against the live env — no `--require-hashes`, no `-r <locked-file>`, no `--locked`. The `.pip-audit-ignore` file does not even exist yet (it's referenced conditionally with `if [ -f ]`).

Let me assess the accuracy of the assessor's technical claims about pip-audit's pinned-aware modes, and whether the "non-reproducible audit" framing holds.

                 [--fix] [--require-hashes] [--index-url INDEX_URL]
                 [-r REQUIREMENT | --path PATH | project_path]

content.text:
All claims are now verified against live evidence. I have everything needed to render judgment.

**Verification summary:**

1. **No lockfile/constraints/requirements anywhere** — confirmed (find returned zero results across the tree, .venv excluded).

2. **The audit job installs unpinned and audits the live env** — confirmed (ci.yml:88 `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"`; ci.yml:95 bare `pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES`, no `-r`, no `--require-hashes`, no `--locked`).

3. **pip-audit supports the pinned-aware modes the assessor cites** — confirmed in the venv's own help: `--locked`, `--require-hashes`, `-r/--requirement` all exist. So the claim that these "cannot be used" without a locked file is accurate.

4. **Large fast-moving surface** — confirmed: 230 dists in the venv, cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, etc. (slightly higher than the 217 the assessor cited; consistent with non-pinned drift, which is itself the point).

5. **This is the unaddressed half of Pass-1 #8** — confirmed. Pass-1 #8's fix delivered part (a) the pip-audit job; part (b) ("Consider also committing a constraints/lock file... so installs are reproducible and audited") was explicitly worded as a softer "Consider also" and was not implemented. So this is NOT a re-report of a fixed finding — it's a genuinely missed sub-item.

**Calibration considerations (against severity):**
- The assessor's core technical claims are all accurate and reproducible.
- The constraints: lockfile reproducibility is unrelated to the hard constraints (loopback/no-metered-API/no-adapter-imports/lazy-backends), so it is not constraint-justified out of scope.
- However, this is a *library* (`assay-engine`, `dependencies = []`, permissive ranges deliberately kept for consumers — pyproject:31, comment at 29-30). For a library, committing a lockfile as the *shipped* dependency contract would be wrong; the assessor correctly scopes this to the *CI heavy lanes only* while "keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers." That's the right shape.
- The actual production risk is moderate: the non-reproducibility means a green audit on commit X today can differ from the same commit tomorrow (transient guarantee), and the audit cannot leverage hash-pinning. But pip-audit IS running and IS gating, so the "no scanning at all" gap (the more serious Pass-1 #8 half) is closed. This residual is the reproducibility/pin-aware-audit hardening layer — real but second-order. MEDIUM is defensible; I lean MEDIUM (consistent with the assessor and with how Pass-1 #8 itself was rated), not lower, because for a self-branded security/privacy engine the inability to reproduce the supply-chain attestation for a given commit is a legitimate production-readiness gap.

input.reasoning:
TRUE finding, correctly scoped, MEDIUM confirmed. Every concrete claim is verified against live code/tooling, and this is the genuinely unaddressed half of Pass-1 #8 (not a re-report of a fixed item).

WHAT WAS FIXED by Pass-1 #8: the `audit` CI job (ci.yml 78-95) now runs `pip-audit` with an `.pip-audit-ignore` allowlist hook. That closes the "no scanning at all" gap.

WHAT REMAINS (this finding): Pass-1 #8's own suggested fix also said "Consider also committing a constraints/lock file ... so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers." That second half was never implemented. Confirmed:
1. No lockfile/constraints/requirements exists anywhere — `find` (excluding .venv) for *.lock/uv.lock/poetry.lock/constraints*.txt/requirements*.txt returns zero results.
2. The audit job installs UNPINNED and audits the live env, not a locked file: ci.yml:88 `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` (fresh resolution every run), ci.yml:89-95 runs bare `pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES` — no `-r <file>`, no `--require-hashes`, no `--locked`.
3. The assessor's claim that pip-audit's pinned-aware modes are unusable without a locked file is accurate: the venv's own `pip-audit --help` lists `--locked`, `--require-hashes`, and `-r/--requirement` — all of which require a constraints/lock file to be meaningful.
4. The large, fast-moving transitive surface is real: 230 dists in the extras venv (assessor said 217+; drift itself reinforces the point), incl. cryptography 48.0.1 and aiohttp 3.14.1 as cited.

IMPACT (why it is real, not style): For an engine self-branded for security/privacy data being hardened for production, the audit attestation is non-reproducible — a green `pip-audit` on commit X today can differ from the same commit tomorrow because nothing is pinned (point-in-time snapshot), and the scan cannot leverage hash/lock-aware checking. So the supply-chain gate, while present, gives only a transient guarantee.

SEVERITY = MEDIUM (consistent with how Pass-1 #8 itself was rated; not downgraded): the more severe "no scanning" half is fixed, so this is the residual reproducibility/pin-aware layer (second-order), but it is genuine and not justified by any hard constraint (lockfile reproducibility is unrelated to loopback/no-metered-API/no-adapter-imports/lazy-backends). Not upgraded because pip-audit IS running and gating today, and the resolution drift is bounded by the existing floor+major-cap ranges in pyproject.

SCOPE IS CORRECT: assay-engine is a library (`dependencies = []`, permissive ranges deliberately kept for consumers, pyproject 29-31). A shipped lockfile would be the wrong contract for a library — and the assessor correctly limits the lock/constraints+`--require-hashes` to the CI heavy lanes while keeping pyproject ranges permissive. That is the right fix shape.

MINOR NOTE (not a defect in this finding): the audit job references `.pip-audit-ignore` conditionally (`if [ -f ]`) but the file does not currently exist in the tree; harmless (the conditional handles absence), but worth flagging when the locked-file work lands.

.github/workflows/ci.yml:78-95 — `audit` job: line 88 `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` (unpinned), lines 89-95 bare `pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES` with NO -r/--require-hashes/--locked; line 92 references `.pip-audit-ignore` only `if [ -f ]`.

venv pip-audit --help → exposes `--locked`, `--require-hashes`, `-r/--requirement` (confirms pinned-aware modes need a lock/constraints file).

content.text:
Verified. The finding is TRUE and correctly scoped at MEDIUM.

Key conclusions:
- No lockfile/constraints/requirements file exists anywhere (`find` over the tree, .venv excluded, returns zero).
- The CI `audit` job at `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml:78-95` installs unpinned (`pip install -e ".[dev,all]"`) and runs bare `pip-audit` with no `-r`, `--require-hashes`, or `--locked`, so the scan is a point-in-time snapshot, non-reproducible per-commit.
- This is the genuinely unaddressed second half of Pass-1 #8 (which fixed scanning but deferred the "consider also a lock/constraints file" reproducibility half) — not a re-report and not a documented out-of-scope item.
- The assessor's technical claims all hold: pip-audit's `--locked`/`--require-hashes`/`-r` modes exist in the installed tool and are unusable without a locked file; the extras surface is large (230 dists; cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1).
- Scope is right: assay-engine is a library (`dependencies = []`, deliberately permissive ranges), so the fix correctly targets the CI heavy lanes only, not a shipped lockfile.
- MEDIUM held (not down/upgraded): the more severe "no scanning" gap is already closed, so this is the residual reproducibility layer — real and not constraint-justified, but second-order.

Minor adjacent note: the audit job references `.pip-audit-ignore` (`if [ -f ]`) but that file does not yet exist; harmless today, relevant when the locked-file work lands.
